narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 29.30. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 75.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signaled weaker performance relative to peers compared to the typical student's mid-range decile of around 5.5. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed notably in the same direction, being well below the average of about 5.7, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength. While your LSAT score, though below the dataset average of 36.7, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, it wasn't enough to offset those lower deciles. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly under the typical 3.21, added a small nudge toward the failure prediction, whereas your full-time study plans and family income had minimal sway either way. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than average in key academic metrics that have historically predicted bar exam struggles for students like you, unlike many admitted applicants who show stronger decile rankings and test scores. I know this news is disheartening, especially after your hard work, and it's okay to feel frustrated—rejections like this don't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your high school deciles through additional coursework or explanations if possible, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early. You might also explore bridge programs or community colleges that build academic records tailored for law school success. Keep pushing; many students in similar situations have turned things around with focused improvements. We're rooting for you.
